responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tension lies in balancing methodological rigor with operational feasibility in a novel residential research model. Key trade-offs involve prioritizing data quality against participant comfort, scaling infrastructure without compromising safety, and maintaining ethical standards while advancing computational tools. The selected levers address critical decision points in staffing, data collection, expansion, recruitment, safety, and technology integration, each challenging assumptions about resource allocation, clinical validation, and long-term sustainability.

levers.name: Staffing Structure
levers.consequences: Implementing a rotating shift model with cross-training across roles could enhance coverage flexibility and reduce burnout but risks diluting specialized expertise. Dedicated full-time technicians ensure consistent protocol adherence but may strain budget constraints. Outsourcing non-core functions to external contractors could lower costs but introduces coordination complexity and potential quality variability.
options: Implement a rotating shift model with cross-training across roles
Hire dedicated full-time technicians for each suite
Outsource non-core functions to external contractors
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between operational efficiency and quality control, but the options overlook long-term staff retention strategies and skill degradation risks in cross-training scenarios.

levers.name: Data Collection Prioritization
levers.consequences: Prioritizing low-burden sensors for continuous monitoring maximizes participant compliance but may sacrifice physiological detail. Increasing PSG nights enhances diagnostic precision but raises participant fatigue and operational costs. Integrating real-time anomaly detection algorithms could accelerate event identification but requires robust validation against existing scoring protocols.
options: Prioritize low-burden sensors for continuous monitoring
Increase PSG nights for higher resolution data capture
Integrate real-time anomaly detection algorithms
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses the tension between data comprehensiveness and participant burden, but the options fail to account for potential algorithmic bias in event triage affecting longitudinal pattern analysis.

levers.name: Expansion Thresholds
levers.consequences: Expanding to 12 suites upon pilot success accelerates data collection but risks overextension of safety protocols. Delaying expansion until the 24-month mark ensures stability but may limit early discovery potential. Piloting a hybrid model with external partnerships could distribute risk but introduces dependency on third-party compliance standards.
options: Expand to 12 suites upon pilot success
Delay expansion until the 24-month mark
Pilot a hybrid model with external partnerships
levers.review_lever: This lever balances growth urgency against risk mitigation, but the options neglect potential conflicts between external partnership requirements and internal ethical review processes.

levers.name: Recruitment Strategy
levers.consequences: Broadening inclusion criteria could increase enrollment numbers but may dilute the homogeneity of the parasomnia cohort. Intensifying targeted outreach to specialty clinics enhances precision but depends on external referral rates. Implementing a tiered compensation structure might improve participation rates but raises equity concerns among different participant groups.
options: Broaden inclusion criteria to increase enrollment
Intensify targeted outreach to specialty clinics
Implement a tiered compensation structure
levers.review_lever: This lever navigates the tension between sample diversity and clinical specificity, but the options miss potential disparities in access to care among different demographic groups.

levers.name: Safety Protocol Design
levers.consequences: Enhancing physical barriers with smart monitoring systems improves real-time response capabilities but increases technical complexity. Reducing physical modifications preserves the domestic environment but may compromise safety margins. Introducing peer support systems for participants could improve psychological comfort but requires additional training and oversight.
options: Enhance physical barriers with smart monitoring systems
Reduce physical modifications to maintain domestic feel
Introduce peer support systems for participants
levers.review_lever: This lever manages the tension between safety and environment, but the options overlook the potential for technology-dependent failures in emergency scenarios.

levers.name: Data Sharing Policies
levers.consequences: Expanding data sharing with stricter anonymization could foster broader scientific collaboration but risks re-identification threats. Maintaining current restrictions with controlled access ensures privacy but limits external validation opportunities. Partnering with third-party repositories for curated datasets might balance accessibility with security but introduces external governance dependencies.
options: Expand data sharing with stricter anonymization
Maintain current restrictions with controlled access
Partner with third-party repositories for curated datasets
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses the tension between transparency and privacy, but the options fail to consider the logistical challenges of maintaining dual data management systems.

levers.name: Technology Integration
levers.consequences: Adopting open-source software for data analysis promotes transparency but may lack specialized sleep research features. Investing in proprietary tools for customization ensures compatibility with existing workflows but creates vendor lock-in risks. Collaborating with tech startups for innovation could accelerate development but introduces intellectual property uncertainties.
options: Adopt open-source software for data analysis
Invest in proprietary tools for customization
Collaborate with tech startups for innovation
levers.review_lever: This lever balances innovation against sustainability, but the options neglect the potential for open-source community support to offset proprietary tool limitations.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing ecological validity with technical precision, participant safety with naturalistic behavior, and methodological rigor with operational scalability. Critical levers must address how physical infrastructure, human factors, and ethical constraints interact to shape data quality and research sustainability. Key trade-offs include resource allocation between facility expansion and protocol refinement, the cost of high-fidelity monitoring versus participant compliance, and the tension between proprietary data models and open science principles.

levers.name: Participant Engagement Framework
levers.consequences: Prioritizing immersive on-site programming increases participant retention but risks altering natural sleep patterns through heightened awareness. The focus on structured routines may inadvertently suppress spontaneous parasomnia events, requiring compensatory adjustments in data interpretation protocols.
options: Implement peer-led support groups to foster communal accountability and reduce isolation during extended stays
Introduce personalized sleep environment customization to align with participants' home conditions
Deploy real-time physiological feedback displays to enhance participant understanding of their sleep dynamics
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between therapeutic engagement and ecological validity, but the options miss the risk of cognitive priming effects from continuous physiological feedback

levers.name: Facility Design Optimization
levers.consequences: Adopting modular renovation strategies accelerates initial setup but limits long-term adaptability to evolving monitoring needs. The emphasis on domestic aesthetics may conflict with the need for standardized sensor placement and emergency access routes.
options: Construct adjustable wall panels to reconfigure sleep suite layouts based on monitoring requirements
Integrate biophilic design elements to reduce participant stress without compromising sensor accessibility
Install adaptive lighting systems that simulate natural circadian rhythms while maintaining consistent monitoring conditions
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses spatial efficiency versus functional flexibility, but the options overlook the trade-off between aesthetic priorities and maintenance complexity

levers.name: Data Annotation Workflow
levers.consequences: Implementing dual-rater consensus improves inter-observer reliability but increases annotation latency and resource demands. The emphasis on human oversight may delay algorithm development cycles and create bottlenecks in high-volume data processing.
options: Establish tiered annotation pathways with initial screening by trained technicians followed by specialist review
Develop context-aware annotation templates that adapt to specific parasomnia subtypes
Create a feedback loop where algorithmic suggestions directly inform rater training protocols
levers.review_lever: This lever balances accuracy with efficiency, but the options fail to address the potential for rater bias when algorithmic suggestions influence human judgment

levers.name: Ethical Oversight Protocol
levers.consequences: Instituting community advisory boards enhances public trust but introduces coordination overhead and potential conflicts with institutional review board requirements. The focus on participatory ethics may complicate data sharing agreements with external partners.
options: Form regional ethics councils comprising sleep specialists, patient advocates, and local community representatives
Implement dynamic consent frameworks that allow participants to adjust data usage preferences over time
Create transparent audit trails for all decision-making processes involving participant data
levers.review_lever: This lever manages institutional accountability versus operational agility, but the options neglect the regulatory complexity of cross-border data governance

levers.name: Sensor Calibration Strategy
levers.consequences: Establishing routine calibration schedules improves measurement consistency but increases maintenance workload. The emphasis on technical precision may divert resources from clinical observation and participant interaction.
options: Deploy self-calibrating sensor arrays that adapt to individual physiological differences
Create environmental reference points within each sleep suite to validate sensor accuracy
Implement cross-sensor validation protocols that compare data streams in real time
levers.review_lever: This lever balances technical reliability with operational efficiency, but the options overlook the potential for over-reliance on automated calibration systems

levers.name: Recruitment Incentive Model
levers.consequences: Offering extended stay options increases participant pool diversity but raises costs and logistical complexity. The focus on financial compensation may attract individuals with higher sleep disorder severity than the general population.
options: Provide academic credit opportunities for participants contributing to research training programs
Establish community benefit agreements that reinvest research findings into local healthcare initiatives
Create tiered compensation structures based on participation duration and data quality metrics
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses equity in participation versus cost control, but the options miss the risk of creating unintended selection biases through incentive design

levers.name: Emergency Response Protocol
levers.consequences: Implementing decentralized response teams improves reaction speed but requires extensive staff training and coordination. The focus on rapid intervention may create a culture of heightened surveillance that affects participant behavior.
options: Train all research staff in advanced sleep disorder first-aid and crisis de-escalation techniques
Install location-aware emergency beacons that trigger protocol-specific response sequences
Develop scenario-based simulation drills for rare but critical event types
levers.review_lever: This lever manages safety versus participant autonomy, but the options fail to address the psychological impact of constant monitoring infrastructure

responses.strategic_rationale: The project's core tensions revolve around balancing ecological validity with data precision, participant safety with operational efficiency, and methodological rigor with resource constraints. Critical levers must address the physical environment's dual role as both a research tool and a clinical space, the trade-offs between sensor density and participant compliance, and the ethical imperative to minimize intrusion while maximizing event capture. Key decisions will determine how the facility navigates the interplay between residential comfort, physiological monitoring fidelity, and long-term sustainability.

levers.name: Environmental Comfort Balancing
levers.consequences: Adjusting ambient conditions to optimize participant sleep quality while maintaining sensor sensitivity. Overemphasis on comfort risks compromising physiological data consistency, while excessive clinical rigidity may deter participation or alter natural behavior patterns.
options: Implement adjustable ambient lighting schedules to align with circadian rhythms
Introduce modular furniture configurations to allow participant customization of sleep spaces
Deploy noise-canceling acoustic panels in common areas to minimize external auditory disturbances
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between ecological validity and technical control, but the options overlook the potential trade-off between personalized environments and standardized data collection protocols.

levers.name: Participant-Environment Interaction Design
levers.consequences: Shaping how residents engage with the facility's physical and technological systems. Overly prescriptive interaction models may reduce natural behavior capture, while insufficient guidance could lead to sensor misuse or safety protocol non-compliance.
options: Establish structured daily routines to synchronize participant activity with sensor calibration windows
Create interactive wayfinding systems using tactile markers and audio cues for nocturnal navigation
Develop scenario-based training modules for participants to practice emergency response procedures
levers.review_lever: Prioritizing structured interaction over spontaneous behavior carries hidden costs: none of the options address the risk of habituation affecting long-term data validity.

levers.name: Data Integrity Verification
levers.consequences: Implementing checks to ensure sensor data accuracy across multiple modalities. Overly stringent verification processes may delay analysis timelines, while insufficient validation risks compromising the study's methodological foundation.
options: Conduct nightly cross-modal consistency audits between EEG, actigraphy, and video records
Deploy real-time anomaly detection algorithms to flag conflicting sensor readings
Schedule biweekly manual data reconciliation sessions with independent raters
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses technical reliability versus operational speed, but the options miss the potential for verification protocols to inadvertently alter participant behavior through increased surveillance awareness.

levers.name: Safety Protocol Calibration
levers.consequences: Adjusting physical and procedural safeguards based on real-time feedback. Excessive safety measures may create a clinical atmosphere that suppresses natural behavior, while insufficient protocols risk participant well-being.
options: Implement dynamic lockout mechanisms for exit doors during high-risk sleep stages
Establish tiered response protocols based on sensor-identified behavioral patterns
Introduce periodic safety drills with simulated emergency scenarios
levers.review_lever: Balancing proactive safety with minimal intrusion carries hidden costs: none of the options address the potential for protocol fatigue among staff or participants.

levers.name: Retention Strategy Optimization
levers.consequences: Designing incentives to maintain participant engagement over extended stays. Overly generous compensation may attract non-compliant participants, while insufficient rewards risk attrition and incomplete data sets.
options: Create milestone-based reward structures tied to event capture thresholds
Implement peer support networks between long-stay participants and new admissions
Develop personalized progress reports with anonymized behavioral insights
levers.review_lever: This lever manages financial incentives versus intrinsic motivation, but the options overlook the risk of retention strategies inadvertently influencing self-reported event frequencies.

levers.name: Sensor Network Configuration
levers.consequences: Determining the spatial and functional distribution of monitoring equipment. Overly dense sensor placement may increase false positives, while sparse configurations risk missing critical events.
options: Deploy redundant sensor arrays in high-risk zones to cross-verify abnormal activity
Implement adaptive sensor activation based on participant sleep stage classifications
Create mobile sensor hubs for temporary reconfiguration during extended stays
levers.review_lever: Prioritizing coverage density over simplicity carries hidden costs: none of the options address the maintenance burden of complex sensor networks.

levers.name: Ethical Boundary Negotiation
levers.consequences: Defining limits on data collection and participant observation. Excessive monitoring risks ethical violations, while insufficient oversight may compromise the study's scientific objectives.
options: Establish time-limited data retention policies for non-event recordings
Implement participant-led review panels for sensor placement decisions
Create transparent disclosure protocols for unexpected data findings
levers.review_lever: This lever navigates privacy concerns versus scientific completeness, but the options miss the potential for ethical frameworks to become static rather than adaptive to emerging challenges.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     - "Prioritizing speed over reliability carries hidden costs: none of the options address rollback complexity."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
